content.full_text:
State of California
Fair Political Practices Commission

P.O. BOX 807 • SACRAMENTO, 95804 • • • 1100 K STREET BUILDING, SACRAMENTO, 95814

Technical Assistance • • • Administration • • • Executive/Legal • • • Enforcement • • • Conflict of Interest
(916) 322-5662        322-5660           322-5901          322-6441          322-6444


September 27, 1979


Florence R. Peskoe
Deputy Director
Administrative Office of the Courts
State House Annex CN-037
Trenton, New Jersey 08625

Dear Ms. Peskoe:

     This letter is in answer to your letter dated September 12,
1979, requesting information with respect to financial disclo-
sure by members of the judiciary in California.

     Judges of courts of record have been required since
1976 by California's Political Reform Act to complete and
file Statements of Economic Interests. Other state and
local officials have been required to file since the inception
of the Political Reform Act in 1975. (See Government Code
Section 87200, enclosed.)

     These Statements are filed annually between January 1
and March 1, covering the period of the preceding calendar
year. In addition, Statements must be filed by candidates
for judicial office and persons assuming or leaving judicial
office. (See Government Code Section 87201, 87205.)

     Generally, judges are required to disclose investments
and income from sources in the jurisdiction or who are doing
business, planning to do business or have done business in
the jurisdiction during the past two years, interests in
real property and gifts. Jurisdiction is defined as the
State of California with respect to judges. (See Government
Code Sections 82028, 82029, 82030, 82033, 82034, 82035,
87206-87210.)

Florence R. Peskoe
September 27, 1979
Page Two


The Statements are filed with the clerk of the court
who retains a copy and forwards the original to the Fair
Political Practices Commission. The Statements are reviewed
by the Commission's Documents Examiner to make sure that
they conform on their face with the requirements of the law.

Enclosed are blank Statements of Economic Interests,
Form 720 and Form 721 and their instruction Manuals, a copy
of the Political Reform Act, and a copy of 2 Cal. Adm. Code
Section 18723. The sections which will be most helpful to
you have been marked.

Please call me or Gina Todd at (916) 322-6444 with any
questions you may have regarding this matter.

Sincerely,

[signature]
Ted Prim
Chief, Conflicts of
Interest Division

TP:kp
Enclosures

ADMINISTRATIVE OFFICE OF THE COURTS
STATE OF NEW JERSEY

ARTHUR J SIMMEON,JR.
JUDGE SUPERIOR COURT
APPELLATE DIVISION
ACTING ADMINISTRATIVE DIRECTOR OF THE COURTS
FLORENCE R VISSAGE
DEPUTY DIRECTOR

STATE HOUSE ANNEX
CN-317
TRENTON, NEW JERSEY 08625
609-292-4836

September 12, 1979

Executive Director
Fair Political Practices Commission
1100 K Street
Sacramento, California 95804

Dear Sir:

At the suggestion of Ralph J. Gambell, Director,
Administrative Office of the Courts, California
Judicial Council, I am requesting some assistance
from you.

As you know, there has recently been implemented,
by the Administrative Office of the United States,
rules and regulations regarding financial disclosure
by judicial officers and employees. This has been
required by Public Law 95-521 and applies to:

"All justices and judges appointed to hold
office during good behavior; judges of the
district courts in the Canal Zone, Guam
and the Virgin Islands; judges of the
courts of the District of Columbia;
bankruptcy judges; United States magis-
trates; judicial employees who perform
'adjudicatory functions' (including
bankruptcy judges and U.S. magistrates)
or who are compensated at or in excess
of the minimum rate for grade 16 of the
General Schedule, 5 U.S.C. 5332."

In New Jersey there is no comparable financial
disclosure requirement applicable to the Judicial
Branch nor is there such a requirement with respect
to the Legislative Branch. The Governor has,
by executive order, required such disclosure of
certain of the higher level Executive Branch
officeholders.

- 2 -

For the information of the Chief Justice of
New Jersey, I would appreciate your letting me
know whether in your state there is any requirement with respect to financial disclosure by
officeholders at the state level and, specifically,
whether such a requirement applies to the Judiciary.
If the Judiciary is required to make such financial
disclosure, I would appreciate your explaining,
in as much detail as possible, what the requirements
are, how the program is administered, how long
it has been in effect and other relevant matters.

In order that I might give the Chief Justice
a timely report, I would appreciate hearing from
you, if at all possible, by September 28, 1979.

Very truly yours,

FRP/kk
cc: Ralph J. Gampell

sections.question_synthetic: What are California's financial disclosure requirements for judges and other state officials, how are statements filed and reviewed, and what is the scope of required disclosures?
sections.conclusion_synthetic: California requires judges and state officials to file Statements of Economic Interests annually disclosing investments, income, real property interests, and gifts from sources doing business or planning to do business in the state; statements are filed with court clerks who forward originals to the FPPC for document review.
sections.parsing_notes: No section headers found; LLM: Document contains both the incoming request (dated September 12, 1979) and FPPC's response letter (dated September 27, 1979). No formal QUESTION/CONCLUSION sections present; extracted information from letter body content. Response letter authored by Ted Prim, Chief of Conflicts of Interest Division. Incoming request references federal requirements under Public Law 95-521.

embedding.qa_text:
QUESTION: What are California's financial disclosure requirements for judges and other state officials, how are statements filed and reviewed, and what is the scope of required disclosures?

CONCLUSION: California requires judges and state officials to file Statements of Economic Interests annually disclosing investments, income, real property interests, and gifts from sources doing business or planning to do business in the state; statements are filed with court clerks who forward originals to the FPPC for document review.

embedding.summary: FPPC responds to a New Jersey court administrator's inquiry about California's judicial financial disclosure requirements by explaining that judges have been required since 1976 to file annual Statements of Economic Interests covering investments, income, property, and gifts, with statements filed to court clerks and reviewed by FPPC.
